title: Democracy and Citizenship in South Africa
subject: History

slides.title: Democracy and Citizenship in South Africa
slides.lead: How South Africa became a country where every adult can vote, and what it means to be a good citizen.

media.alt: A hand placing a folded paper into a green ballot box, with a rising sun behind it.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='160' cy='150' r='70' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect y='150' width='320' height='30' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='120' y='96' width='80' height='58' rx='5' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='134' y='86' width='52' height='14' rx='2' fill='#1f3a24'/><rect x='150' y='90' width='20' height='6' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='146' y='54' width='30' height='38' fill='#fff' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2' transform='rotate(-14 160 74)'/></svg>

slides.notes: Begin by asking learners what they think the word 'democracy' means. Write their ideas on the board. Explain that today's lesson tells the story of how South Africa became a democracy in 1994, and what rights and responsibilities citizens have. Keep the tone hopeful - this is a story about people winning the right to be treated equally.

slides.title: What is a democracy?
slides.lead: A democracy is a country where the people choose their own leaders.

blocks.text: The word **democracy** comes from two old Greek words meaning 'rule by the people'. In a democracy, adults **vote** to choose the leaders who make the laws and run the country.

items: Every adult citizen gets one vote, and every vote counts the same.
Leaders are chosen by the people, not born into power.
If people are unhappy, they can vote for different leaders next time.

media.alt: Three raised hands of different colours holding voting slips.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><g><rect x='40' y='70' width='18' height='60' rx='8' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='34' y='50' width='30' height='22' fill='#fff' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/></g><g><rect x='100' y='56' width='18' height='74' rx='8' fill='#b7794a'/><rect x='94' y='36' width='30' height='22' fill='#fff' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/></g><g><rect x='160' y='70' width='18' height='60' rx='8' fill='#8a5a2a'/><rect x='154' y='50' width='30' height='22' fill='#fff' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Use a simple classroom example: if the class had to choose a game to play and everyone put up a hand to vote, that is democracy in action. Contrast it with one person deciding for everyone. Stress the idea of fairness and 'one person, one vote' because it becomes important later in the lesson.

slides.title: When most people could not vote
slides.lead: For many years, unfair laws stopped most South Africans from voting.

blocks.text: In the past, South Africa had a system called **apartheid**, an Afrikaans word meaning 'apartness'. Under apartheid, the law separated people by the colour of their skin and treated them unequally.

items: Only white South Africans were allowed to vote for the government.
Black, Coloured and Indian people had little or no say in the laws that ruled their lives.
Many brave people protested for years to win equal rights for everyone.

media.alt: A fence dividing a page into two unequal parts, one crowded and one empty.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='150' y='20' width='4' height='120' fill='#8a1f1f'/><g fill='#8a1f1f'><rect x='134' y='40' width='4' height='16'/><rect x='166' y='40' width='4' height='16'/><rect x='118' y='60' width='34' height='4'/><rect x='150' y='60' width='36' height='4'/></g><g fill='#7a8b5a'><circle cx='40' cy='100' r='11'/><circle cx='68' cy='96' r='11'/><circle cx='96' cy='102' r='11'/><circle cx='56' cy='120' r='11'/><circle cx='86' cy='122' r='11'/></g><circle cx='186' cy='100' r='11' fill='#c8b89a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Handle this gently and honestly. The message for Grade 6 is that a law once treated people unfairly because of their skin colour, and that this was wrong. Reassure learners that people worked hard and peacefully to change it. Avoid graphic detail; keep the focus on fairness and the right to be heard. Link to the Bill of Rights later, which was written to make sure this never happens again.

slides.title: Leaders sit down to talk
slides.lead: In the early 1990s, South Africa's leaders met to plan a fair, democratic country.

blocks.text: Leaders from many groups came together at meetings known as **CODESA** (the Convention for a Democratic South Africa). Instead of fighting, they talked about how to share power and hold a fair election for all South Africans.

items: Nelson Mandela had been released from prison in 1990 after 27 years.
The leaders agreed that everyone aged 18 and older should be allowed to vote.
They planned the country's very first democratic election.

media.alt: Two people shaking hands across a round table.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><ellipse cx='110' cy='118' rx='78' ry='20' fill='#c8b89a'/><circle cx='64' cy='58' r='16' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='52' y='74' width='24' height='36' rx='6' fill='#2f5233'/><circle cx='156' cy='58' r='16' fill='#b7794a'/><rect x='144' y='74' width='24' height='36' rx='6' fill='#3f6b45'/><rect x='84' y='94' width='52' height='10' rx='5' fill='#e8a13a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Emphasise the power of dialogue: South Africa avoided a full-scale war because leaders chose to negotiate. Name Nelson Mandela and F.W. de Klerk as two of the important figures, and note they later shared the Nobel Peace Prize. Keep it simple - the key idea is that talking and compromise built the new democracy.

slides.title: The first democratic election
slides.lead: On 27 April 1994, South Africans of every race voted together for the first time.

blocks.text: On **27 April 1994**, millions of people stood in long, patient queues to vote. For most black South Africans, it was the very first time they were ever allowed to choose their government.

items: The African National Congress (ANC) won the election.
**Nelson Mandela** became South Africa's first democratically elected President on 10 May 1994.
27 April is now a public holiday called **Freedom Day**.

media.alt: A long line of people waiting to vote next to a ballot box.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='240' height='32' fill='#7a8b5a'/><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='40' cy='70' r='10'/><rect x='33' y='82' width='14' height='40'/><circle cx='68' cy='68' r='10'/><rect x='61' y='80' width='14' height='42'/><circle cx='96' cy='70' r='10'/><rect x='89' y='82' width='14' height='40'/><circle cx='124' cy='68' r='10'/><rect x='117' y='80' width='14' height='42'/></g><rect x='170' y='72' width='46' height='50' rx='4' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='182' y='64' width='22' height='10' rx='2' fill='#5c1414'/><rect x='189' y='66' width='8' height='4' fill='#f4efe6'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is the heart of the lesson. Paint the picture of the long queues - some people waited many hours and were joyful to vote at last. Explain that Mandela had been in prison for fighting apartheid, and now became President. Connect Freedom Day to learners' own lives: they get a holiday each year to remember this moment.

slides.title: The Constitution of South Africa
slides.lead: The Constitution is the most important set of rules in the country - even the government must obey it.

blocks.text: After the election, South Africa wrote a brand-new **Constitution** in 1996. A constitution is the **highest law** of a country. It explains how the government works and lists the rights of every person.

items: President Mandela signed the Constitution at Sharpeville on 10 December 1996.
It came into use on 4 February 1997.
No law and no leader is allowed to break the rules in the Constitution.

media.alt: An open book at the top of three steps, showing it is the highest law.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='24' y='128' width='172' height='16' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='52' y='112' width='116' height='16' fill='#b7a98d'/><rect x='80' y='96' width='60' height='16' fill='#9aa77f'/><path d='M78 60 q32 -14 62 0 v34 q-30 -12 -62 0 Z' fill='#2f5233'/><line x1='109' y1='58' x2='109' y2='94' stroke='#1f3a24' stroke-width='3'/><rect x='92' y='40' width='36' height='6' rx='3' fill='#e8a13a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Use the staircase image to explain 'highest law' - it sits above all other laws. Mention that Mandela deliberately signed it at Sharpeville, a place where people had been killed protesting apartheid, to show a new beginning. Keep dates light; the key idea is that the Constitution protects everyone and even the President must follow it. [SME] Confirm the 4 February 1997 commencement date and the Sharpeville signing detail against a primary source before production.

slides.title: The Bill of Rights
slides.lead: The Bill of Rights is a special part of the Constitution that protects every person in South Africa.

blocks.heading: Equality
blocks.text: Everyone is equal and must be treated fairly, whatever their race, language or religion.

blocks.heading: Dignity
blocks.text: Every person must be respected and treated with kindness.

blocks.heading: Education
blocks.text: Every child has the right to go to school and learn.

blocks.heading: Safety
blocks.text: Everyone has the right to be safe and to be cared for.

blocks.heading: Freedom of speech
blocks.text: People may share their ideas and opinions freely.

blocks.heading: A clean environment
blocks.text: Everyone has the right to a healthy environment, now and in the future.

slides.notes: The Bill of Rights is Chapter 2 of the Constitution. Read each card aloud and ask learners for an example from their own lives (for instance, the right to education means they may attend school). Emphasise that these rights belong to everyone equally - this is exactly what apartheid denied. You can pick just three or four cards if time is short.

slides.title: Elections in South Africa now
slides.lead: South Africa holds elections so citizens can keep choosing their leaders.

blocks.text: Every few years, South Africans vote in an election. A group called the **Independent Electoral Commission (IEC)** makes sure the election is free and fair.

items: Citizens must be 18 or older and must register to vote.
Voting is **secret**, so no one can be forced to vote a certain way.
The votes are counted carefully, and the party with the most support forms the government.

media.alt: A ballot paper with a tick in a box and an ink-marked thumb.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='44' y='28' width='96' height='104' rx='4' fill='#fff' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='56' y='44' width='40' height='6' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='56' y='60' width='72' height='6' fill='#e6ddcb'/><rect x='104' y='58' width='22' height='22' fill='#fff' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M108 70 l6 6 l10 -14' fill='none' stroke='#2f5233' stroke-width='4'/><rect x='56' y='92' width='72' height='6' fill='#e6ddcb'/><ellipse cx='168' cy='104' rx='16' ry='22' fill='#7a5a3a'/><ellipse cx='168' cy='90' rx='12' ry='10' fill='#3a3f6b'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the IEC as the 'fair play referee' of elections. The inked thumb in the picture shows how voters are marked so no one votes twice. Reinforce the idea of a secret ballot - your vote is private, which keeps voting fair and free from bullying. Note that this happens regularly, so leaders must keep listening to the people.

slides.title: Rights and responsibilities
slides.lead: Being a citizen means you have rights - and also duties to others.

items: To be treated equally and with respect.
To go to school and learn.
To be safe and cared for.
To say what I think.
Respect other people's rights too.
Obey fair laws and rules.
Look after our environment.
Help and care for my community.

slides.notes: This is a key CAPS idea: rights come with responsibilities. Draw the link across the two columns - your right to be treated with respect means you must respect others; your right to a clean environment means you must not litter. Ask learners to add one more responsibility of their own to the list.

slides.title: The three levels of government
slides.lead: South Africa is governed at three levels that work together.

blocks.text: The people we vote for work at three levels of government. Each level looks after different jobs, from the whole country down to your own town.

items: **National** government looks after the whole country and works from Parliament in Cape Town.
**Provincial** government runs each of the nine provinces.
**Local** government (the municipality) looks after your town, water, roads and rubbish collection.

media.alt: A pyramid with three layers labelled national, provincial and local.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M110 26 L150 66 L70 66 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='64' y='70' width='92' height='30' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect x='40' y='104' width='140' height='34' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='96' y='44' width='28' height='4' fill='#fff'/><rect x='92' y='82' width='36' height='4' fill='#fff'/><rect x='86' y='118' width='48' height='4' fill='#fff'/></svg>

slides.notes: Keep this concrete for Grade 6 by linking each level to real life: national makes the big laws, provincial runs schools and hospitals, and local government is why there is water in the tap and rubbish is collected. There are nine provinces - see if learners can name their own province and its capital.

slides.title: Key dates to remember

blocks.alt: A timeline from 1990 to 1996 showing steps to democracy.

events.label: Nelson Mandela is released from prison
First democratic election on 27 April; Mandela becomes President

slides.notes: Use the timeline to help learners put the story in order - a common CAPS skill. Ask them to say what happened at each date in their own words. Point out how quickly things changed: just four years from Mandela leaving prison to South Africa having a new Constitution.

slides.title: How can you be a good citizen?
slides.lead: You do not have to wait until you are 18 - you can be a good citizen right now.

blocks.text: A **citizen** is a member of a country who belongs there and shares in looking after it. Even as a young person, you can help build a fair and caring South Africa every day.

items: Treat everyone with kindness and fairness, no matter who they are.
Keep your school and community clean and safe.
Follow fair rules and speak up politely when something is unfair.
Learn about your country's history and its Constitution.

media.alt: Children of different backgrounds planting a tree together.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='126' width='220' height='34' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='104' y='84' width='10' height='42' fill='#7a5a3a'/><circle cx='109' cy='72' r='24' fill='#2f5233'/><circle cx='60' cy='96' r='12' fill='#b7794a'/><rect x='52' y='108' width='16' height='24' rx='5' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='160' cy='96' r='12' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='152' y='108' width='16' height='24' rx='5' fill='#e8a13a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Bring the lesson home to learners' own choices. Democracy is not only about voting once every few years - it is about how we treat each other every day. Invite the class to promise one thing they will do this week to be a good citizen, such as helping a classmate or picking up litter.

slides.title: South Africa belongs to all
slides.lead: The Freedom Charter of 1955 dreamed of the democracy South Africa built in 1994.

blocks.text: South Africa belongs to all who live in it, black and white.
This promise, made long before democracy, came true in the new Constitution. It reminds us that South Africa is a home for every person who lives here.

slides.notes: Read the quote together and unpack it in simple words: the country belongs to everyone equally. Explain that this idea was written down in 1955, long before 1994, and that people held onto this dream for decades until it became real. This links citizenship back to shared belonging.

slides.title: What we learned
slides.lead: Democracy gives every citizen a voice, protected by the Constitution and the Bill of Rights.

blocks.text: We learned that South Africa became a **democracy** in 1994 when everyone could vote, that the **Constitution** is the highest law, and that citizens have both **rights and responsibilities**.

blocks.question: What happened on 27 April 1994, and why was it so important?
Name two rights from the Bill of Rights and explain why each one matters.
Describe two responsibilities you have as a citizen of South Africa.

slides.notes: Use the three questions as a written task or a class discussion. They follow the CAPS style of recall plus short explanation. Encourage learners to use full sentences and the new words from the lesson: democracy, Constitution, Bill of Rights, citizen, rights and responsibilities. Close by reminding them that they are all citizens with a part to play.
